Because the value combines FAH1 and CYP84A1, only FAH1 is explicit; accession-style synonym support is partial.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Materials and Methods explicitly states A. thaliana / Arabidopsis thaliana.

Enzyme_Name.reason: The title and text explicitly name FERULATE 5-HYDROXYLASE.

EC_Number.reason: No EC number for F5H appears in the paper.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: The paper cites prior work describing F5H as a cytochrome P450-dependent monooxygenase in references, but CYP84A1/family designation is not explicitly stated in this paper text.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: F5H is discussed throughout as part of the phenylpropanoid/monolignol pathway affecting lignin composition.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: The paper states F5H overexpression shifts lignin from G toward S units, supporting assignment to the syringyl monolignol branch.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: Results and introduction describe F5H overexpression redirecting flux from G- to S-monomers and reducing CA while increasing sinapaldehyde/sinapyl alcohol, supporting this reaction description at a functional level.

Primary_Substrate.reason: The paper supports that F5H overexpression draws down coniferyl alcohol-related pools, but does not directly define the enzyme's exact substrate(s) here.

Primary_Product.reason: Table 1 and Results show F5H overexpression increases sinapaldehyde and sinapyl alcohol, consistent with 5-hydroxylated intermediates leading to these products.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: The paper treats F5H as a downstream branch-switching step that alters G/S composition rather than an early core entry step.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: The central model in Results/Discussion is that F5H overexpression diverts flux away from coniferyl alcohol-derived products, contributing to pinoresinol deficiency.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The paper supports that F5H strongly controls G/S lignin composition, but does not explicitly call it rate-limiting or a key control step.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: ER/microsome localization is not stated in this paper.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Materials and Methods and Discussion state the C4H promoter drove the F5H overexpression construct.

Applied_Promoters.reason: The transgene is explicitly written as C4H:F5H and discussed as C4H promoter-driven.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: Pinoresinol is directly tested and identified as a rescue metabolite.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: The paper explicitly states G(8-8)G, also known as pinoresinol, is a lignan.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Results show pinoresinol rescues growth and lateral root defects; CA rescues root hair morphology and the broader model links these metabolites to normal development.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Results directly show F5H overexpression causes severe growth defects in ref3, ccr1, cadc/cadd backgrounds, reduces lateral roots in CAD-related backgrounds and F5H lines, and causes altered root hairs in cadd C4H:F5H/fah1-2 C4H:F5H.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: These methods are all present in the Results: genetic comparisons, metabolite profiling, chemical complementation with CA and pinoresinol, and lateral root phenotyping.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: CA, pinoresinol and related dimers/lignans, and NAA auxin treatment were explicitly applied.

Omics_Data.reason: The paper explicitly reports untargeted LC-MS metabolomics, [12C6]/[13C6] CA labeling, PODIUM LC/MS, and LC-MS/MS.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: This is a reasonable implication from the study, but not explicitly stated as an application claim in the Results.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Results explicitly report dwarfism, sterility in some backgrounds, reduced lateral roots, and abnormal root hairs associated with F5H overexpression in mutant backgrounds.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The paper explicitly states F5H overexpression alone mainly shifts lignin composition with little growth effect, but in phenylpropanoid mutants exacerbates defects; it also shows complementation is not due to major transgene suppression.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All three summary points are directly consistent with Results and Discussion.
